turns.thought: The swarm has completely collapsed: all particles have identical fitness (near 7.26e-10) and zero velocity. They are extremely close to the true optimum (sphere function), but the collapse in diversity prevents any further improvement. A drastic reset (e.g., reinitializing worst particles) could damage the near-optimal solution. Instead, a gentle Gaussian mutation on a subset of particles will add small position and velocity noise, enabling fine-grained exploration around the current best. This re-introduces diversity without losing the current high-quality region. The scale is kept small (1% of search span) to keep particles near the optimum. Targeting random particles (since all are equivalent) and mutating half the swarm should break the stagnation.
turns.observation: Action 'gaussian_mutation' was applied. Applied Gaussian mutation to 15 particles. Best cost changed from 7.259412e-10 to 7.259412e-10 (delta=+0.000000e+00).

turns.thought: The Gaussian mutation successfully reintroduced velocity and positional diversity, breaking the velocity collapse. Although the global best did not immediately improve, the swarm now has the ability to move and continue local fine-tuning towards the optimum. Since the sphere function is unimodal and the current best is extremely close to zero (7.26e-10), small perturbations are exactly what is needed. Further intervention now could risk losing progress. No additional action is required; the PSO dynamics should now converge to the global minimum.
turns.observation: Action 'none' was NOT applied (no particles changed). Best cost remains 7.259412e-10.
